lecture_title: Stanford CS231n Lecture 11: Large-Scale Distributed Training

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture introduces the necessity of large-scale distributed training in modern deep learning, noting a paradigm shift over the last decade. Previously, models were trained on a single GPU, but the current norm involves training on tens, hundreds, or even tens of thousands of devices concurrently. The instructor uses Meta's Llama3-405B as a running example because, unlike closed models (such as GPT-4, which notoriously published no architecture or training details in its 2023 paper), the Llama 3 technical report openly shares deep system infrastructure and parallelization strategies. This openness provides a valuable look into state-of-the-art LLM training.
key_takeaways: Large-scale distributed training on thousands of GPUs is the new industry standard for deep learning.
Meta's Llama3-405B serves as an excellent case study due to its open technical report detailing hardware and distributed training methods.
The industry has shifted towards secrecy regarding model architectures and training setups, making open reports highly valuable for learning.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: This section dives into the physical hardware powering AI, specifically the NVIDIA H100 GPU. Originally designed for graphics, GPUs have evolved into general-purpose parallel processors. The H100 architecture reveals a strict memory hierarchy: 80GB of High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) surrounds the compute cores, with a smaller 50MB L2 cache and 256KB L1 cache closer to the logic units to optimize bandwidth. A single H100 contains 132 active Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) out of 144 built (due to manufacturing yield 'binning'). Each SM houses 128 FP32 cores (for general scalar math) and 4 Tensor Cores. The Tensor Cores are specialized circuits that execute mixed-precision matrix multiplications (e.g., 16-bit inputs, 32-bit accumulation) in a single clock cycle. This dedicated hardware is responsible for the massive throughput of modern GPUs. Over the past decade, from the K40 to the upcoming B200, GPU computational power has increased by roughly 1000x.
key_takeaways: Modern GPUs rely heavily on a tiered memory hierarchy (HBM, L2, L1) to manage data flow efficiently.
Tensor Cores are specialized hardware units within the GPU that perform rapid, mixed-precision matrix multiplications, providing the bulk of the device's computational throughput.
GPU computing capability has scaled by 1000x over the last 10-15 years, serving as the primary driver for recent AI advancements.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Moving beyond a single GPU, the lecture outlines the physical infrastructure of a massive data center, framing it as 'one giant supercomputer.' The network topology is hierarchical. A standard server contains 8 GPUs communicating internally at ~900 GB/sec. Two servers are stacked into a physical rack (16 GPUs). Multiple racks form a Pod (e.g., Llama 3 used pods of 192 racks, totaling 3,072 GPUs), where GPUs communicate at a lower bandwidth of ~50 GB/sec. Finally, multiple pods are connected to form the cluster (e.g., 24,576 GPUs for Llama 3). Other hardware alternatives exist, notably Google's Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), which follow a similar pod-based scaling strategy, as well as emerging chips like AMD's MI325X and AWS Trainium. A key theme is the drop in communication bandwidth as data travels further up the hierarchy (from inside a GPU, to inside a server, to across a pod).
key_takeaways: A modern AI data center acts as a single, massively parallel supercomputer.
Communication bandwidth drops significantly as data moves from chip-to-chip, to server-to-server, to pod-to-pod.
Algorithms must be explicitly designed in software to respect and optimize for this physical network topology.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Data Parallelism (DP) is introduced as the foundational strategy for distributed training, exploiting the linearity of gradients. In DP, the global minibatch is split across M GPUs. Each GPU holds a full replica of the model weights, optimizer states, and gradients. Each GPU independently loads its unique microbatch of data, runs a forward pass to compute local loss, and runs a backward pass to compute local gradients. Because gradients are linear, the true global gradient is simply the average of these local gradients. To synchronize, the cluster performs an 'All-Reduce' operation, gathering and averaging the gradients across all GPUs. Once all-reduced, every GPU applies the exact same weight update. Crucially, the gradient communication can be overlapped with the backward pass computation (e.g., broadcasting layer L+1's gradients while computing layer L's backward pass) to hide the slow network communication speeds.
key_takeaways: Data Parallelism involves splitting the batch of data across GPUs, while each GPU maintains a complete replica of the model.
Global gradients are calculated by averaging local gradients via an All-Reduce operation.
To maximize efficiency, the communication of gradients is overlapped asynchronously with the backward pass computation.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Data Parallelism breaks down for very large models because a single GPU cannot store the entire model state. A 100B parameter model requires ~800GB of memory (assuming 16-bit precision and Adam optimizer states), far exceeding an H100's 80GB capacity. Fully Sharded Data Parallelism (FSDP) solves this by splitting the model weights, gradients, and optimizer states across the GPUs. Each weight matrix is 'owned' by a specific GPU. During the forward pass, the owner broadcasts the weights to all GPUs; once the layer's computation is done, non-owning GPUs delete the weights to save memory. In the backward pass, weights are broadcasted again to compute local gradients, then non-owning GPUs send their gradients to the owner, which aggregates them and updates the weights. This requires significantly more communication (broadcasting weights twice, plus sending gradients) but allows the training of models far larger than a single GPU's memory.
key_takeaways: FSDP overcomes the memory limitations of standard DP by sharding model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across all GPUs.
Weights are dynamically broadcasted layer-by-layer during forward and backward passes, and immediately deleted to free memory.
FSDP trades increased network communication for massive memory savings, enabling the training of models with hundreds of billions of parameters.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Because FSDP requires extensive communication, it becomes inefficient across low-bandwidth network connections (like inter-node or inter-pod links). Hybrid Sharded Data Parallelism (HSDP) addresses this by creating a 2D parallelism grid. The cluster is divided into smaller groups of GPUs with high interconnect bandwidth (e.g., 8 GPUs inside a single server). FSDP is applied strictly within these high-bandwidth groups, meaning the model is sharded across 8 GPUs. Then, standard Data Parallelism (DP) is applied across the different groups over the slower network links. This minimizes the heavy weight-broadcasting traffic to the fast intra-node links, while only requiring gradient all-reduces over the slower inter-node links, effectively balancing memory constraints with network topology.
key_takeaways: HSDP combines FSDP and DP to optimize for the physical topology of the data center.
FSDP is localized to groups of GPUs with fast interconnects to handle heavy communication efficiently.
Standard DP is used across groups to minimize traffic over slower network links.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Even with FSDP, the memory required to store forward pass activations for the backward pass can exhaust GPU memory, especially for deep networks with long sequences (e.g., Llama 3 405B requires 63GB just for FFN hidden states). A naive solution is to discard all activations and recompute them from the beginning for every layer during the backward pass, but this requires an unacceptable O(N^2) compute time. Activation Checkpointing provides a middle ground: the system saves a 'checkpoint' of the activations only every C layers (often set to C = sqrt(N)). During the backward pass, intermediate activations are recomputed locally from the nearest checkpoint. This clever trade-off reduces memory requirements from O(N) to O(sqrt(N)), at the cost of only about 33% extra forward-pass computation.
key_takeaways: Storing all forward pass activations for deep networks exceeds GPU memory limits.
Activation checkpointing discards intermediate activations and recomputes them on-the-fly during the backward pass.
Saving checkpoints periodically (e.g., every sqrt(N) layers) optimizes the trade-off, severely reducing memory usage for a minor compute penalty.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Hardware and Model FLOPs Utilization (HFU & MFU)
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: With countless parallelism knobs to tune, engineers need a metric to measure efficiency. Hardware FLOPs Utilization (HFU) measures what percentage of a GPU's theoretical peak throughput is achieved by raw matrix multiplications (often reaching ~80% on H100). However, HFU ignores overhead from data loading, communication, activation recomputation, and non-linearities. Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) is the true 'guiding light' metric. It is calculated by taking the theoretical FLOPs strictly required for a standard forward/backward pass of the model architecture, divided by the actual total time a training iteration takes. MFU reveals the true efficiency of the distributed setup. An MFU above 30% is considered good, and above 40% is excellent. Paradoxically, newer GPUs (like H100 vs A100) sometimes show lower MFU because compute power scales faster than network communication bandwidth, widening the communication bottleneck.
key_takeaways: HFU measures raw matrix multiplication efficiency, while MFU measures the true end-to-end efficiency of the training loop.
MFU is the ultimate metric used to tune distributed training setups; >40% MFU is state-of-the-art.
Newer GPUs often yield lower MFU percentages because their massive compute speed exposes the slower scaling of network bandwidth.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: When scaling to extremely massive models (>50B parameters) and massive context windows, DP and FSDP are insufficient. The lecture introduces three advanced techniques operating along the remaining tensor dimensions. Context Parallelism (CP) splits along the sequence dimension, crucial for handling sequences of 100k+ tokens; methods like Ring Attention and Ulysses (parallelizing over attention heads) are used to distribute the tricky attention matrix. Pipeline Parallelism (PP) splits along the layer dimension, placing different sequential layers on different GPUs. To avoid 'bubbles' where GPUs sit idle waiting for sequential data, PP interleaves multiple micro-batches simultaneously. Finally, Tensor Parallelism (TP) splits the model dimension itself, distributing a single weight matrix's computation across GPUs via block matrix multiplication. TP is highly efficient in Transformers because consecutive operations (like the two layers of an MLP) can be chunked by columns then rows, avoiding intermediate communication.
key_takeaways: Context Parallelism divides long token sequences across GPUs, requiring complex attention partitioning like Ulysses or Ring Attention.
Pipeline Parallelism divides network layers across GPUs, utilizing micro-batches to keep all GPUs actively computing and avoid idle 'bubbles'.
Tensor Parallelism splits individual weight matrices to run single operations in parallel, exploiting block matrix math to minimize communication.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: In practice, state-of-the-art models do not rely on just one parallelism strategy; they use all of them simultaneously, resulting in 'ND Parallelism' (Multi-Dimensional Parallelism). A prime example is the Llama3-405B training run, which arranged 16,000 GPUs in a 4D grid, utilizing 8-way Tensor Parallelism, 16-way Context Parallelism, 16-way Pipeline Parallelism, and 8-way Data Parallelism all concurrently. The goal of this complex orchestration is to perfectly align the diverse communication requirements of each parallelism type with the physical network topology of the cluster (e.g., placing heavy TP communication on fast NVLink connections inside a node, and lighter DP communication across slower pod connections). By carefully tuning these parameters, engineers can maximize Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) and efficiently train behemoth models.
key_takeaways: State-of-the-art training utilizes Multi-Dimensional (ND) Parallelism, applying DP, FSDP, CP, PP, and TP simultaneously.
Parallelism axes must be mapped to the physical cluster hierarchy to hide slow communication times behind compute operations.
Optimizing the intersection of hardware topology and software algorithms is required to maximize MFU and train modern LLMs.
